frameworks.name: nearest-neighbor statistics
frameworks.one_line: Quantifies behavioral change without hand-picked features or discrete classes

frameworks.name: repertoire dating
frameworks.one_line: Assigns each rendition a pseudo production-day from neighborhood typicality

frameworks.name: feature-based syllable analysis
frameworks.one_line: Traditional hand-picked features assuming discrete syllable classes

core_question: How can vocal learning and developmental change be quantified without assuming discrete syllable classes or hand-picked features?
key_assumptions: Nearest-neighbor statistics in behavioral space capture meaningful change
Behavioral repertoire varies continuously rather than in discrete classes
Long-term learning-related variability is separable from short-term variability
manipulations: Tracked song renditions across days of juvenile development
Compared each rendition against the full repertoire distribution
key_findings: Pseudo production-day isolates variability congruent with long-term vocal learning
About 10% of renditions have pseudo production-days >10 days into the future (anticipations)
Similar fraction shows renditions falling >10 days into the past (regressions)
Vocal variability is large relative to developmental trajectory
interpretations: Song acquisition involves complex non-monotonic dynamics with anticipations and regressions
Bias-free neighborhood statistics generalize across behaviors and species better than feature-based methods
